did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper conducted a comparative analysis of three AI-based smartphone applications (ProRodinki, Skinive, and SkinVision) for self-examination of skin cancer risk. The study utilized neural networks within these apps to process 516 histologically confirmed skin images and calculated sensitivity and specificity metrics to evaluate their performance in detecting malignant vs. benign neoplasms.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'Research article' in the header. It describes a 'practical experiment' involving the processing of 516 histologically confirmed skin images using three specific AI-based smartphone applications (ProRodinki, Skinive, and SkinVision). The study presents original results, including sensitivity and specificity metrics calculated from a confusion matrix, which classifies it as an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: Comparison analysis of AI-based smartphone applications for self-examination of skin cancer risk
brief_summary: This study conducted a comparative analysis of three AI-based smartphone applications—ProRodinki, Skinive, and SkinVision—for self-examination of skin cancer risk. The researchers processed a dataset of 516 histologically confirmed skin neoplasm images through each application to evaluate their diagnostic performance. The main evaluation approach involved calculating sensitivity and specificity metrics based on binary classification results derived from a confusion matrix. The key results showed that SkinVision achieved the highest sensitivity (94.71%) but the lowest specificity (44.98%), while ProRodinki demonstrated the best balance with 85.19% sensitivity and 93.95% specificity. Skinive exhibited lower sensitivity (60.00%) but high specificity (92.88%). The authors concluded that there is a current lack of standardized methodology for testing such AI applications, which hinders objective comparison.

lesion_types_studied.details: Group consisting of Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and Melanoma. Total count: 188 cases (~36% of dataset).
Group consisting of Nevus, Hemangioma, and Seborrheic keratosis (SK). Total count: 328 cases (~64% of dataset).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin neoplasms into malignant (positive) or benign (negative) categories.
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates AI smartphone applications for self-examination of skin cancer risk. The primary task is binary classification, where the models predict whether a skin lesion is malignant or benign. The paper explicitly states that 'confusion matrix for binary classification is used for each application study' with two class labels: malignant (positive) and benign (negative). Results are reported as sensitivity and specificity metrics derived from True Positives, False Negatives, False Positives, and True Negatives. Although the apps may internally assess 'risk levels' (e.g., low, medium, high), the study aligns these to a common binary format for comparison, treating the output as a diagnostic category prediction rather than a continuous regression target.

datasets_used.name: Histologically confirmed skin neoplasm dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset consisting of 516 images of skin neoplasms and pathologies confirmed by histological research. The images were not part of the training datasets for the AI applications being tested. Images were displayed on a 13.3-inch screen (2560x1600 resolution) and photographed with a smartphone camera for processing by the apps.

use.testing: Used as the test set for the practical experiment to evaluate the diagnostic performance (sensitivity and specificity) of three AI-based smartphone applications (ProRodinki, Skinive, and SkinVision).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study presents a comparative analysis of sensitivity and specificity metrics derived from confusion matrices for three AI-based smartphone applications without statistical significance testing.

metric: Confusion Matrix Values (TP, FP, TN, FN)
Histologically confirmed skin neoplasm d.notes: The study processed 516 images (188 malignant, 328 benign). ProRodinki demonstrated the best balance of sensitivity and specificity. SkinVision had the highest sensitivity but lowest specificity. Skinive had the lowest sensitivity but high specificity. Some images were unprocessed by certain apps (see Table 2 in paper for failed screenings).

architecture_used.main_model: Proprietary neural networks specific to each application (ProRodinki, Skinive, SkinVision). The paper does not disclose the specific model architectures (e.g., CNN variants, transformers) used by these commercial applications.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported. The paper states that the dataset images were not part of the training datasets for the neural networks scoped for the research, but does not detail the pretraining data or methods of the apps.
architecture_used.input_size: Not reported. Images were displayed on a 13.3-inch screen (2560x1600 resolution) and photographed with a smartphone camera, but the internal input dimensions processed by the neural networks are not specified.

architecture_details.ProRodinki: Neural network built and trained on several thousand diagnosed cases, working under permanent dermatology expert supervision.
architecture_details.Skinive: Clinically proven technology; recognizes 51 skin pathologies. Specific architecture details not disclosed.
architecture_details.SkinVision: Regulated medical service using neural network analysis. Specific architecture details not disclosed.

training_details.ProRodinki: Trained on several thousand diagnosed cases.

classification_or_output_head.ProRodinki: Binary risk assessment (likelihood of melanoma and basal cell skin cancer) with recommendations to visit a doctor.
classification_or_output_head.Skinive: Risk assessment and detection of 51 skin pathologies; outputs low/medium/high risk levels.
classification_or_output_head.SkinVision: Risk assessment with follow-up recommendations monitored by real doctors.

preprocessing_techniques: Image quality validation/checks performed by each application's internal algorithms before processing
Mole localization and detection (highlighting detected moles or using AI mode to detect and highlight mole area with a red square)
Alignment of risk assessment results to a common binary format (classifying low/medium risk as low risk for comparison purposes)
Neutral presentation of mole prescription data and other details to standardize input conditions across apps
conclusions: ProRodinki demonstrated the best balance of sensitivity (85.19%) and specificity (93.95%) among the tested applications.
SkinVision achieved the highest sensitivity (94.71%) but the lowest specificity (44.98%), likely configured to minimize the risk of missing malignant neoplasms and associated legal risks.

Skinive exhibited the lowest sensitivity (60.00%) but high specificity (92.88%), with a high number of false negatives potentially attributed to the challenge of training neural networks for a large nomenclature of 51 skin pathologies.
Each AI-based smartphone application is unique in its principles of work, algorithms, user experience, design, AI model, and input data analysis.
There is a current lack of standardized methodology for testing and analyzing different AI-based smartphone applications, which hinders objective and transparent comparison.
AI-based smartphone applications cannot replace dermatologists and oncologists but serve as tools to provide advice and guidance.
The study highlights the importance of follow-up by professional doctors, particularly for applications like SkinVision where AI results are monitored by real doctors to mitigate low specificity.
important_remarks: The study lacks a standardized methodology for testing AI-based smartphone applications, which hinders objective and transparent comparison.
Results may be distorted because images were photographed from a 13.3-inch display (2560x1600 resolution) rather than captured directly in clinical settings.
SkinVision exhibited a significant trade-off with high sensitivity (94.71%) but very low specificity (44.98%), likely configured to minimize legal risks associated with missing malignant cases.
Skinive showed low sensitivity (60.00%) with a high number of false negatives (66), potentially due to the challenge of training neural networks on a large nomenclature of 51 skin pathologies.
Not all dataset images were successfully processed by all applications; specific counts of failed screenings are reported (e.g., Skinive failed on 42 cases, SkinVision on 36).
The dataset is imbalanced, consisting of 328 benign (~64%) and 188 malignant (~36%) histologically confirmed cases.
The study does not report statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals) for the comparative metrics.
AI applications cannot replace dermatologists and oncologists; they serve only as advisory tools, and professional follow-up is crucial, especially for apps with lower specificity.
ProRodinki demonstrated the best balance of sensitivity (85.19%) and specificity (93.95%) among the tested applications.
Risk assessment results from different apps were aligned to a common binary format for comparison, treating low/medium risk as low risk, which may obscure nuanced clinical advice.
